questions.a: (1) Each value in Rust has exactly one owner. (2) There can only be one owner at a time. (3) When the owner goes out of scope, the value is dropped (Drop is called).

questions.q: What is the difference between a move and a copy in Rust? Name one type that is copied and one that is moved on assignment.
questions.a: A copy duplicates the value on the stack; both bindings remain valid. A move transfers ownership; the original is no longer accessible. `i32` is copied; `String` is moved.

questions.q: State Rust's two borrow rules and explain how they prevent data races.
questions.a: Rule 1: You can have any number of immutable references (&T) at a time. Rule 2: You can have at most one mutable reference (&mut T), and no immutable references at the same time. Together, these ensure no two threads can read-and-write the same location concurrently.

questions.q: When should a function parameter be `&str` instead of `&String`, and why?
questions.a: `&str` is preferred because deref coercion allows it to accept both string literals (`&'static str`) and references to owned Strings (`&String` → `&str` automatically). Using `&String` unnecessarily restricts callers to only owned Strings.
